data.title: Flowserve Corporation: Capitalize On Booming End-Market Trends And Solid Execution
data.description:
Flowserve Corporation (NYSE:FLS) is a leading provider of flow control systems and equipment, making it vital to the smooth functioning of facilities and plants in various industries. The company has been seeing stellar topline growth, owing to end market demand and other external trends. On the internal side, its efforts to expand its margin, and strengthen its financial position are encouraging. I believe FLS is very clearly undervalued when compared to its peers. I rate the stock as a buy.

Overview

In the present age, flow is critical in processing plants and production facilities throughout a range of markets and industries. By flow, I specifically mean that of various fluids and material, the precise control and management of which is critical to smooth and efficient functioning. Whether it is cooling, heating, mixing, or transporting, many industries invest substantial resources in optimizing their flow systems.

This is where Flowserve enters the picture. A $7.2 billion company, in terms of market cap, and a leader in the flow control systems space. Flowserve entered the scene in 1997, with the merger between Durco International and BW/IP, two high profile fluid motion specialist companies. Today, its products and services are critical to some of the most critical global infrastructure industries, which include oil and gas, water management, as well as power production (including nuclear, renewables, and fossil fuels).

More recently, FLS products are proving crucial in two distinct areas. Firstly in decarbonization efforts where the company's cutting-edge pump and valve systems are aiding in the reduction of carbon capture in an energy-efficient manner. Secondly, the company is working with its clients to digitize its operations to ensure optimized operations and a more transparent flow control.

Flowserve uses a dual approach to segment its revenue streams. First, it does so by product type. The first is Flowserve Pump Division (FPD), which focuses on industrial fluid pumps, mechanical seals and related auxiliary systems. This division makes up about 70% of the company's total sales. The second category is that of Flow Control Division (FCD), through which the company offers control valves, isolation valves, and valve automation systems.

While the FPD and FCD classification is based on the nature of offerings, Flowserve also segments its revenue by the nature of sales: original equipment sales, and the aftermarket segment, which consists of post-sales replacement, maintenance, and related services. By this segmentation, the company's revenues show a nearly 50-50 split. Bifurcation in this manner allows for a clearer picture into the company's performance, as we shall see in the next section.

Q2 Assessment

The company's second quarter results were positive, showing growth by nearly all metrics. On the topline, FLS saw a YoY climb of 7.08%, and delivering total sales of $1.16 billion. While the company saw strong growth in both its product segments, in original equipment sales and in aftermarket sales, the real star was the FCD side of the business, particularly in terms of its original equipment sales.

I have tabulated the YoY change on the company's revenue breakdown in the table below, for a clearer picture:

In addition to the strong growth seen in nearly all the company's divisions, bookings have also increased by 12% to $1.25 billion in the quarter, compared to Q2 in the prior year. These are the highest booking figures the company has seen since 2014. This is indicative of strong demand and continued topline growth in the future. As per Q2, Flowserve's total backlog is stands at a whopping $2.7 billion.

On the margin side, things looked just as robust, with a 204 basis point YoY increase on the gross margin front, whereas operating margin surged by 162 basis points in the same time. This margin expansion is a part of a broader trend seen in the last two years, which is a promising sign:

This gradually expanding margin trend is a part of Flowserve's broader 'excellence program', as mentioned in its recent earnings call, where the company is strategically focused on identifying opportunities to enhance its margins. So far, as part of its commitment to the program, the company initiated a rigorous portfolio review in one of its seven business units, and has already identified areas to be optimized to improve margins. Flowserve aims to expand this program towards all of its business units across the enterprise in the upcoming quarters, and add up to 200 basis points of margin improvement before 2027. The results it has achieved so far are already promising and hint at the potential improvement likely to be sustained in the upcoming years.

During Q2, FLS made strong strides on the operational front, with its operating income climbing by 23.78% YoY. This strong performance also translated to the bottom line and the EBITDA, which each saw increases of 42% and 20%, respectively.

In addition to its internal efforts that are proving to be quite positive, I am also positive about the company's prospects based on external trends. For instance, the oil and gas end markets proved to be the largest drivers for the company's growth, with its original equipment seeing rising demand in various plants and facilities in this industry. On a YoY basis, oil and gas actually brought in a revenue jump amounting to 22%. I believe demand from this front will remain robust in the long term.

After oil and gas, chemical markets came in at second place, driving 14% YoY growth on the topline in Q2. Although the main driver for this growth was the Emerald greenfield petrochemical project in Saudi Arabia, the broader trend in the gulf region towards petrochemical projects bodes quite well for FLS.

Additionally, I must also add that Flowserve, remarkably also holds exposure to the booming demand in computational power, coming about as a result of increasing AI application activity. In fact, during Q2, the company saw a 34% jump in bookings related to the power generation space, which has been driven by rapidly growing data center capacity. Other notable areas where the company sees growing activity, which is pushing demand for its equipment and services, is in the mining space as well as in nuclear power generation.

I find it extremely promising that FLS has built itself up in a manner such that its offerings give it exposure to so many booming market trends. I believe the company is very well positioned to capitalize on the booming trends in the global markets, and its strong backlog is testament to the robust demand it enjoys.

Finally, although the company has a substantial long-term debt of $1.2 billion, I am encouraged by the trend it has been following since 2015, of widening the gap between equity and debt:

Financially, this not only puts the company in a less vulnerable position in the event of demand slowness and interest rate climbs, but also allows it to re-invest its gains into growth opportunities, rather than paying interest on its debt.

Valuation

While there may be a number of distinct approaches to take in order to determine whether FLS is fairly valued or not, for the purposes of this analysis, I will go with the EV/EBITDA ratio. Specifically, I opt for a peer group analysis, comparing how the market is valuing different players in the 'Industrial Machinery & Supplies & Components' space, in terms of the forward EV/EBITDA ratio, and the forward EBITDA growth rate each of these peers show.

Each of the peers in this analysis have an enterprise value ranging from approximately $7 to $10 billion, so they are operating at a similar scale, which makes for a fair comparison, in my opinion.

As can be seen on the scatter graph above, FLS is a very prominent outlier to the general relationship between EV/EBITDA and EBITDA growth rate. Its growth rate of almost 30% makes it the highest among its peers yet its EV/EBITDA ratio is comparatively really low. To me, this points to a very attractive entry point. I rate the stock as a buy.

Risk

While I did mention the company's broad exposure to so many end-markets as a strength, it can also be a risk for the company when certain end markets show signs of slowness. This could limit the company from achieving its topline growth targets. For instance, oil and gas facilities remain among the largest customer type for FLS, so a slowdown in this end market could see demand for its original equipment take a plunge. I believe there are signs that oil demand will slow down, as confirmed by the recent statement by OPEC.

Similarly, while I believe gas processing in the Middle East represents a strong opportunity for the company, this will likely be significantly offset by the demand drop amid oversupply in European markets. This creates a situation where producers will not be looking to expand output, and hence would be unlikely to invest in additional equipment and systems for their plants. So while the broader trends do look promising, there are a few limiting factors to keep an eye on, which could offset the gains the company makes.

Investor Takeaway

With its advanced flow system offerings, Flowserve is critical to a number of industries across the globe, which include oil and gas, nuclear power generation, water management, and a lot more. Owing to booming growth in its various end markets, the company's offerings are seeing strong demand, as is evident by its topline climb and record bookings. On the internal side, I am encouraged by the company's sustained efforts towards expanding its profit margins, and solidifying its financial position. I believe this trend will likely continue throughout the long term. Compared to its peers in the industrial machinery and component space, FLS boasts the highest growth prospects at the lowest EV/EBITDA ratio, leading me to giving the stock a buy rating.

data.title: Tidefall Capital Management Q2 2024 Letter
data.description:
There are few words that investment partners would like to hear less in 2024 than "we recently purchased a Chinese stock", yet here we are. As a Western investor, China is well known for its high level of geopolitical risk and more recently its weakening economy and property market. However, we believe there is one investment that is so compelling that it makes the risk worth the potential reward.

Pinduoduo (PDD) is an online market place that is unlike anything we have ever seen before in terms of efficiency, viral growth and cut throat capitalism. PDD is growing so fast that a dedicated sell side analyst admitted in their latest quarterly report, which saw PDD grow revenue by 131% and net income by 202%, that it was likely the 'biggest beat' that they had ever seen in their career.

Started in 2015, PDD's initial platform innovated 'team buying' via social media, whereby the more friends you added to a purchase, the lower the price would become. PDD turned their buyers into their marketers which not only dramatically lowered their customer acquisition cost but also rapidly increased the purchasing volumes they could offer merchants. Furthermore, in a move that's similar to TikTok, PDD leveraged its data to algorithmically adjust the product page to dramatically increase user engagement and retention. Instead of just searching for a product, the app had an uncanny ability to show consumers what they were most likely to buy, even if they didn't know they needed it.

When PDD says they remain committed to providing customers with the lowest cost, they mean it. Instead of just promising a lowest price guarantee like Western retailers, PDD employs an automatic price tracking system that instantly lowers a seller's price if it is found to be cheaper on another platform. Merchants tolerate such brutal tactics due to the large amount of volume that PDD offers with its approximately one billion active users. In less then a decade, PDD has grown into the second largest online retailer in China.

This is a stunning accomplishment given Alibaba's (BABA) strong lead in what is the most mature e-commerce market globally. PDD has an incredibly fast moving, flat management structure, with US$35bn in sales and just 17,000 employees, it's staying lean as it grows; its revenue per employee metric has quadrupled in the past five years.

What's most interesting for Western investors was the September 2022 launch of Temu which was PDD's move into international markets. Temu's initial growth hack in the US was by shipping directly from China and keeping orders under $800, it could avoid US tariffs that bulk semi-hosted peers like Amazon (AMZN) had to pay. A January 2023 Bernstein analysis showed a median 32% price savings on Temu vs Amazon for nearly identical items. The app went viral after Temu's 2023 Super Bowl ad which told Americans that they could 'shop like a billionaire', a message that resonated strongly with lower income consumers. Similar to PDD's initial China strategy, the app offered discounts for bringing on new users. The company continued to invest into their Temu fly-wheel with an estimated $2bn in Facebook (META) ads. Temu quickly became the most downloaded app in the US in 2023, a position it retains to this day. The marketing dollars appear well spent as Temu's customer retention is double that of Walmart (WMT), Target (TGT) and even direct Chinese peers like Shein. Temu wasn't the first to identify the direct import loophole, (Shein and Wish had been taking advantage of it for over a decade), but Temu was able to execute best and became the biggest online discount retailer in the US in less than a year.

Although the initial advertising campaign and generous subsidies at Temu meant that PDD was running their international operations at a loss, most analysts now believe the company is breaking even on a gross profit basis.

There are certainly risks to owning a Chinese stock, especially as both US political candidates seem all but certain to significantly increase Chinese tariffs and even a potential Temu ban is not without question. We would counter that any tariff increase will be applied to all retailers, including Amazon. Secondly, Temu has been aggressively moving to a semi-hosted model (similar to Amazon) that does not rely on the tariff loophole and will greatly decrease shipping times. Interestingly, Amazon is now trying to copy Temu's direct shipping strategy but we believe they will struggle to be as efficient as PDD and their consumers, trained for years to expect overnight shipping will be frustrated by the more than week long delivery times. Finally, although the US is Temu's largest market, it is no longer the majority of its revenue and ultimately at today's valuation and given the value of PDD's aggressively expanding China business, investors are receiving Temu's optionality at zero cost.

As impressed as we are with the incredible execution of PDD, what made us willing to wade into the difficult waters of China was the incredible valuation of the shares. At the end of the second quarter and adjusting for current unrestricted cash, PDD was trading for just 8x 2025 GAAP estimates, with forecasted revenue growth of 29% and immense long term earnings potential as it increase its low ~6% take rate. While we are mindful of the risks, we view PDD as worthy of an appropriately sized position in the fund.

Trevor Scott

This document is intended for informational purposes and should not be construed as an offering or the solicitation of an offer to purchase an interest in Tidefall Capital Management LP (the "Fund"). Past performance should not be mistaken for and should not be construed as an indicator of future performance and there is no assurance that the investment objectives of the Fund will be achieved. An investment in the Fund involves a high degree of risk. The information contained in this document is not, and should not be construed as, legal, accounting, investment or tax advice. The contents of this document are based upon sources of information believed to be reliable but no warranty or representation, expressed or implied, is given as to their accuracy or completeness. All opinions and estimates contained in this report constitute the Manager's judgment as of the date of this report, are subject to change without notice and are provided in good faith but without legal responsibility. The Manager asserts that the reader is solely liable for their interpretation and use of any information contained in this document.

Original Posted on July 16, 2024

data.title: Billionaire Says Global Conditions 'Are Treacherous And Getting Worse': Our Approach
data.description:
Billionaire and JPMorgan Chase (JPM) CEO Jamie Dimon recently emphasized the tremendous geopolitical risks facing the economy and market. He stated:

We have been closely monitoring the geopolitical situation for some time, and recent events show that conditions are treacherous and getting worse. The outcome of these situations could have far-reaching effects on both short-term economic outcomes and, more importantly, on the course of history.

In this article, we will discuss what these risks are and the implications that they have for the global economy and stocks, and then share how we are positioning our portfolio as a result.

Geopolitical Risks & Implications

Mr. Dimon highlighted that the global order (which is essentially built upon the preeminent strength of the U.S. military - and its Navy in particular - and has facilitated the rapid rise of globalization and free trade) is now in peril. If a growing alliance between Russia, North Korea, China, and Iran succeeds in undermining the U.S.-enforced global economic order, it could cause a dramatic unwinding of much of the wealth built up over the past several decades, potentially leading to a global depression and a massive meltdown in stock markets worldwide.

Additionally, there is a significant risk that a conflict involving China and Taiwan would likely result in a prolonged delay in many of the promising developments from artificial intelligence (AI), as much of the AI supply chain runs through both China and Taiwan, which is aligned with the U.S. A conflict between China and Taiwan would be especially disruptive, severely impacting global semiconductor and technology supply chains given that so much of the world's semiconductor manufacturing takes place in Taiwan (TSM). This in turn would wreak havoc on leading technology companies like NVIDIA (NVDA), Apple (AAPL), and Tesla (TSLA) and cause challenges for many other companies worldwide. This risk is particularly acute given that China and the U.S. are the two leading powerhouses in AI development globally.

Moreover, the risks of war over Taiwan are growing, especially since Taiwan's president recently emphasized the country's right to independence in a National Day speech, asserting that the Chinese Communist Party has no right to rule over Taiwan given that Taiwan's government predated the Communist Party's rise to power. Unsurprisingly, this angered Xi Jinping and the Chinese government, given that a Taiwanese declaration of independence is a red line for them and could lead to a war. As a result, they launched significant military drills around Taiwan in response to the Taiwan President's speech.

Furthermore, China's ongoing dispute with the Philippines could escalate into open conflict and involve the U.S., given the 1951 Mutual Defense Treaty that commits the U.S. to defend the Philippines. Even if such a conflict in the South China Sea did not spread to other parts of the world, it could still cause massive disruptions to global trade, as approximately 60% of global maritime trade flows through the region.

On top of that, the war in Ukraine continues to escalate, with North Korea recently sending thousands of troops to assist Russia. Some of these troops have already sustained casualties, leading Ukrainian leader Volodymyr Zelenskyy to announce that North Korea has officially joined the war against Ukraine. Moreover, Russia recently approved a draft budget boosting defense spending by 25% from already elevated 2024 levels, signaling a commitment to continuing the war with no signs of an imminent peace agreement.

Last but not least, the proxy conflict between Israel and Iran is also escalating into increasingly direct military engagement between the two regional powers. Following a deadly airstrike in Beirut, Iran launched more than 118 missiles at Israel, and there are growing concerns that Israel will retaliate by striking strategic Iranian assets. Another major flash point is the conflict between North and South Korea, which could erupt into full-scale war at any moment, given the recent escalation of rhetoric and exchanges of fire between the two sides. Additionally, Japan is rapidly building up its military in response to threats from North Korea, Russia, and China.

When these developments are considered alongside the contentious U.S. election this fall, there are numerous catalysts that could destabilize the global economy and stock markets.

Our Approach

Given these risks, how are we positioning our portfolio?

First and foremost, we have been very bullish on gold (GLD) over the past year and have enjoyed attractive returns from our initial call on the yellow metal:

And our aggressive investments in miners like Newmont Corporation (NEM) earlier this year.

We also doubled down on Virtu Financial (VIRT) earlier this year, recognizing its undervaluation and ability to profit from market volatility, such as during the 2020 COVID crash. This investment has also paid off as VIRT has been a tremendous winner for us this year.

We believe that farmland also makes an excellent safe haven investment as it is defensive in nature, limited in supply, and should only increase in value if geopolitical conflicts disrupt global supply chains. Farmland Partners (FPI) is an attractive play on this right now, given its steep discount to its net asset value, management's efforts to sell off non-core properties at reasonable valuations, and the recycling of proceeds from these sales into debt reduction and share buybacks at steep discounts to NAV. Additionally, the preferred shares of Gladstone Land Corporation (LAND) (LANDP) offer a 7% dividend yield from preferred equity that is both safe and defensive in nature.

We also are investing in stagflation-resistant stocks - those that are defensive in an economic downturn and built to withstand elevated inflation. This is because global conflicts are likely to create stagflationary conditions by disrupting trade and supply chains, pushing the economy into recession, while government spending and printing to support wars as well as shortages from supply chain disruptions could drive up prices.

For this, we favor quality defensive infrastructure businesses with inflation-protected contracts, such as Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) and Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP) (BEPC). Both companies offer attractive dividend yields, strong dividend growth, and investment-grade balance sheets, along with long-term contracted cash flows with inflation-linked escalators that generate stable profits regardless of macroeconomic conditions.

Investor Takeaway

By diversifying a portfolio with picks that benefit from geopolitical headwinds-such as gold-related investments, market makers like VIRT, safe-haven assets like farmland, and blue-chip stagflation-resistant dividend growers like EPD and BEP-we believe it will be well-positioned to outperform the broader market and protect value in the event of major geopolitical conflicts.

data.title: Alcoa Stock Looks Interesting On The Dip (Upgrade)
data.description:
My Updated Thesis

I reinitiated my coverage of Alcoa Corporation (NYSE:AA) in April 2024, stating that Alcoa seemed to be a sensible beneficiary of sanctions against its largest competitor, Rusal. I anticipated an increase in aluminum prices globally due to these sanctions, coupled with relatively strong global demand, which should boost Alcoa's revenue growth in the medium term. My bullish call generally aged well, as Alcoa stock has performed comparably to the S&P 500 index (SP500) since then (at higher volatility, though):

The company reported its Q3 2024 earnings on October 16, 2024. It partially exceeded analysts' forecasts, with actual EPS beating the consensus by about 72%, which is significant, in my opinion. On the contrary, the sales figure fell short of expectations (albeit marginally). Initially, the stock responded with strong growth in the pre-market session, but it soon fell sharply and closed lower, now trading well below its pre-report value.

I believe this dip presents a good opportunity to buy the stock today, as the positives I mentioned earlier for Alcoa still hold, and it's likely that aluminum prices will remain above 2020 levels or those seen during most of 2021-2022.

The fact that the company exceeded Q3 forecasts that much suggests to me that the market isn't accurately assessing the firm's profit growth prospects over the coming quarters - this presents an opportunity for investors, as it indicates that Alcoa's current valuation may become significantly underestimated at some point.

My Reasoning

According to the press release for Q3 2024, Alcoa's aluminum output rose for the 8th quarter in a row, with record capacity at the Mosjoen smelter in Norway and greater stability at the Alumar smelter in Brazil, which has reached close to 80% capacity. Yes, Alcoa's revenue remained flat sequentially at $2.9 billion, but the company's net income was up $90 million from $20 million in the previous quarter, and its EPS increased to $0.38. Adjusted net earnings were $135 million ($0.757/share) and adjusted EBITDA rose $130 million to $455 million. The higher EBITDA was driven by "improved realized third-party alumina prices, lower energy and raw material costs, and lower other expenses" (albeit lower metal prices).

Alcoa's acquisition of Alumina Limited on August 1st significantly broadened its economic exposure to the aluminum market, where prices have reached their highest levels since 2018. This purchase was accretive to the Q3 earnings, excluding net income from non-controlling interests and saving approximately $100 million in cash tax payments over the next 12-18 months. In addition, AA initiated the sale of its 25.1% share in the Ma'aden joint ventures (for about $1.1 billion) expected in the first half of 2025 as part of its move to reshape and streamline its portfolio.

According to the management, Alcoa will continue to increase alumina volumes and grow the aluminum business. They're anticipating a positive impact from the rising shipments and lower production costs in the 4th quarter, but they also foresee some hurdles, like higher alumina costs in the aluminum division and higher costs associated with equity losses in Ma'aden. Nevertheless, the strong third quarter prompted analysts to revise their forecasts for the upcoming quarters - this trend has been particularly noticeable over the past month.

Controlling cash flow was paramount in Q3; AA's CapEx and working capital conversions were the two largest expenditures. With inventory and accounts payable increasing, working capital increased in the third quarter; even so, Alcoa recoups substantial savings under the profitability improvement programs and raised $525 million of the targeted $645 million in Q3. The company also earned positive returns on equity of 0.6% and remained at a cash flow of $1.3 billion, which is good news for investors.

As I noted in my previous article, the geopolitical landscape in the world has evolved significantly since April 2021, and Alcoa can still reap from changing trends in the global aluminum market. Just to bring some context, Russia and Ukraine are at war and sanctions are heavily impacting Rusal, Alcoa's largest competitor. Those sanctions, particularly in the UK and the US, could impact Rusal's sales by up to 36%, cutting sales by 1.5 million tons annually. This is just like the global financial crisis in 2008 when demand collapsed and Rusal was forced to reduce production. But now the demand isn't really collapsing. While Rusal is finding it hard to stay ahead of the game, particularly in the EU, Alcoa is likely to make the most of aluminum prices and increased demand from customers who need alternatives to Russian metal.

Even as aluminum prices continue to rise, demand continues to grow around the world, and this is reflected in Alcoa's recent results.

I find Alcoa's volume growth projections reflect strength against market volatility. While rising prices might in theory slow demand, manufacturers seem to be getting used to the new pricing landscape, and demand remains high. This sets Alcoa up to possibly produce more than current market expectations. Rusal will lose market share in this regard due to sanctions, so having steady supply chains and product origination traceability adds value for customers, in my view. Alcoa's bargaining advantage with this is that it's one of the world's largest producers and can now seize further market share in semifinished and value-added products - such advantage coupled with management's focus on profitability makes Alcoa a bright medium-term beneficiary.

Superimposing the dynamics of the EBITDA margin on the stock price chart, we see a clear correlation, even if there are lags here and there. According to the current analysts' consensus forecast (YCharts data), Alcoa's EBITDA margin will rise to 15.5% next year, compared to the current TTM figure of 10.15% - theoretically, this growth should be directly reflected in a rise in the stock price. Moreover, the margin should be even higher in 2 years (at 16.3%), so the upside potential should be sustainable.

Alcoa is currently trading at a forward EV/EBITDA of 9.4x, which is approximately the median for the materials sector, according to Seeking Alpha.

However, from a historical perspective, I believe that with the current tailwinds and the company's strong margins, Alcoa's EBITDA margin could potentially double from here. So the market's current expectations might be overly pessimistic, leading to Alcoa being undervalued today, especially considering its global expertise and presence.

I expect Alcoa to achieve a 17% EBITDA margin by the end of 2025. Assuming the current consensus revenue growth forecast of 3.03% YoY to $11.89 billion is accurate, we have an EBITDA of just over $2 billion. If the stock trades at only 7x EV/EBITDA, which aligns with the long-term median, and we adjust the resulting EV for the net debt of $1.62 billion, we arrive at a fair market cap (equity value) of ~$12.53 billion. This figure is approximately 20.3% higher than the company's current market cap.

Based on the above, I believe AA deserves an upgrade to "Buy" after its recent dip on a strong Q3 report.

Risks To My Thesis

Overall, I feel quite confident about Alcoa, but a few risky scenarios could throw a wrench in my numbers. For example, the geopolitical dynamic that currently favors Alcoa due to the sanctions against Rusal could turn against the company. Once relations improve or sanctions are lifted, Rusal could regain market share and reduce Alcoa's lead in particular regions. In addition, geopolitical issues in other markets could disrupt Alcoa's supply chains or increase prices and reduce profitability.

The other risk is aluminum price volatility. While I don't predict a drop in prices, major declines could hurt Alcoa's revenues and margins. An ailing global economy, lower demand from key sectors, or stronger production in other international markets could lead to a decline in aluminum prices - this would be enough to make it difficult for Alcoa to maintain its recovery growth and could discourage investors from investing in the company.

Finally, operational issues within the company could also add to Alcoa's risk profile. Although recent performance has been positive, failures to optimize the production process, control costs or the strategic plan could limit the company's ability to take advantage of good market conditions. In addition, if Alcoa is unable to integrate the acquisition of Alumina Limited, the expected benefits may not materialize, which would impact the company's earnings.

Your Takeaway

Despite the risks, I think Alcoa makes for an appealing investment as the company's recent performance - including a significant earnings surprise in Q3 2024 - suggests that it can take advantage of the commodity price of aluminum and demand expansion as rivals such as Russian Rusal struggle with sanctions. Although the recent dip in the stock price has occurred, Alcoa's positive operational metrics - with a hefty boost to aluminum production and higher profitability - indicate that the stock market might not have fully priced in its potential growth. Having a forward EV/EBITDA ratio similar to the sector median, I think the actual figure may be even less (I assume a higher EBITDA margin), making Alcoa seem like a bargain considering its global reach and experience. I believe AA can continue to provide long-term value to investors, so I rate the stock as a "Buy" after its post-earnings dip, which I find unjustified.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: ClearBridge International Value Strategy Q3 2024 Commentary
data.description:
By Sean Bogda, CFA, Grace Su & Jean Yu, CFA

Fiscal Easing Boosts International Stocks

Market Overview

International markets generated positive returns in the third quarter as rate cuts and financial easing from global central banks helped spark a rotation into value sectors such as real estate, utilities and communication services. Additionally, a late-quarter surge by Chinese stocks proved helpful to more cyclical sectors including materials, industrials and consumer discretionary. The benchmark MSCI EAFE Index showed positive returns across nine of its 11 sectors, with only energy and information technology declining. Value outperformed growth during the quarter, with the MSCI EAFE Value Index returning 8.89% versus the 7.26% return of the MSCI EAFE Index and the 5.68% of the MSCI EAFE Growth Index (Exhibit 1).

Exhibit 1: MSCI Growth vs. Value Performance

Despite the Bank of Japan having telegraphed its intention to raise interest rates for quite some time, the size of its rate hike in August caught investors off guard, leading to a spike in market volatility on concerns that tightening of economic policy would pressure its nascent recovery. This was further exacerbated by a violent unwinding of the pervasive yen carry trade as the currency strengthened during the quarter, all of which culminated in Japan being the worst-performing region in the benchmark. Despite the increase in market volatility, we are seeing signs that Japanese businesses have become more efficient and profitable - in part due to improving corporate governance measures - and are beginning to share these gains with employees in the form of the largest wage increases in over a decade, which will hopefully translate into greater consumer confidence and spending.

International investors were not disappointed when the Federal Reserve elected to cut rate by 50 basis points at its September meeting, sparking a strong rally in U.S. markets and a rotation into smaller value and cyclical stocks. With the first rate cut in the rear view, the question now shifts from "when" to "how much," with policymakers carefully watching as inflation appears to be coming back in line with its target and whether the U.S. economy can indeed stick a soft landing. Investors now look toward the U.S. presidential election in November, rampant with speculation as to what the permutations of political math would mean on policies surrounding international policy, trade and immigration.

"With the first rate cut in the rear view, the question for the Fed now shifts from 'when' to 'how much?'"

The European Central Bank (ECB) and Bank of England also cut interest rates during the quarter, the second cut in three months for the ECB and the first in four years for the BoE, as the U.K. and eurozone central banks have embarked on a more measured approach considering the slow growth rates in their respective economies. In the U.K., the snap election and landslide victory of the previously opposition Labour Party in July has resulted in a significant shift in political direction as well as economic priorities and policies. While the U.K. economy continues to hold up more strongly than those across the Channel, this new direction has spurred greater uncertainty as to the details on what these new policies would mean for the economy and markets.

While businesses and consumers continue to painfully adjust to an economy no longer tethered to property development and globalization, Chinese stocks surged late in the quarter following the announcement of an unusually broad stimulus package aimed at revitalizing the world's second-largest economy. The People's Bank of China announced that it would cut both policy interest rates and mortgage rates and, along with its existing plan to lower bank reserve ratios, set aside billions in stimulus funds for loans to brokers and insurers to purchases equities and help listed companies finance share buybacks. While the tone of Chinese government officials seems more determined than in prior iterations of policy support, there remains a considerable amount of skepticism from economists and investors that these initial efforts can translate into a durable and sustained turnaround for the broader Chinese economy.

Quarterly Performance

The ClearBridge International Value Strategy outperformed its benchmark in the third quarter as strong performance from our holdings in the consumer discretionary and financials sectors overcame headwinds from our energy exposure.

In consumer discretionary, the combination of macro tailwinds and strong idiosyncratic drivers helped spur outperformance for Spanish retailer Industria de Diseno Textil (OTCPK:IDEXY, Inditex) and U.K.-based catering company Compass Group (OTCPK:CMPGF). Inditex, a fast fashion, footwear and accessories retailer, continues to show strong operational execution despite softer consumer trends in Europe. The company's investments in technology and building out both its ecommerce and omnichannel operations, store refurbishments and supply chains continue to reap the rewards of creating a differentiated and positive experience for its customers versus its competitors. Compass Group has also been able to show strong performance, delivering higher growth rates relative to its pre-COVID levels, due to the company's strategic focus on growing its outsourcing business and targeting underdeveloped markets in Europe where it can benefit from its size and strength.

Financials also proved beneficial, particularly European and U.K. banks with strong, well-known franchises. Our leading contributor in the sector, U.K.-based Lloyds Banking (LYG), provides a wide range of banking and financial services globally and continues to use its well-capitalized balance sheet to its advantage despite a challenging economic environment. Likewise, BAWAG (OTCPK:BWAGF), an Austrian bank, continues to perform well and completed two acquisitions during the period - Barclays Consumer Bank Europe and Dutch bank Knab - which will expand the bank's strong brand presence across Europe.

Oil prices declined significantly in the third quarter, as signs of rising inventory levels in the U.S., lackluster demand from China and the prospect of further production increases from OPEC+ members outweighed investor concerns about a widening of Middle East conflicts. These broad-based headwinds weighed on the performance of our exploration and production holdings Shell (SHEL) and Noble (NE). However, we continue to have high conviction in these names as Shell has been able to maintain strong operating metrics despite the downturn. Likewise Noble, a leading offshore drilling contractor, continues to have a strong strategic position as the leading provider of drilling ships, which we believe has ramping earnings potential when demand rebounds.

Stock selection in Japan was also a positive contributor to performance, driven by strong year-to-date contributor Hitachi (OTCPK:HTHIY) and relatively new holding Fujitsu (OTCPK:FJTSF) - a Japanese IT services firm. Heavy reliance on technology during the COVID-19 pandemic highlighted the need for more investment and modernization in IT services for both consumers and businesses, creating a multiyear wave of investment that directly benefits Fujitsu. Hitachi has also benefited from demands for investment and modernization in areas like power grids. Additionally, the company closed the acquisition of Thales' rail signaling business, which we believe will create synergies that should improve the profitability of its domestic systems integration business.

Portfolio Positioning

We added a new position in utilities company National Grid (NGG), which transmits and distributes electricity and national gas in the U.K. and U.S. The current investments in AI and data centers will undoubtably also increase demand for energy and, with the company's shares currently trading at a historical discount, we believe that the market is overlooking National Grid's attractive long-term outlook, improved balance sheet and footprint within favorable regulatory environments.

We also added SONY, the Japanese electronic equipment and devices conglomerate. While a cyclical slowdown in demand for the company's image sensors has weighed on recent earnings, it has also created a compelling entry price to gain exposure to its attractive games and gaming platform segments, including the PS5 gaming console. Additionally, we believe that the divestiture of its financial services business will result in a greater focus on core strengths within its content businesses.

We exited our positions in Inpex (OTCPK:IPXHF), a Japanese energy company engaged in oil and natural gas production, and Metso Oyj (OTCPK:OUKPF), a Finnish industrials company that provides solutions and services for aggregates, minerals processing and metal refinement industries. Inpex reached our price target during the quarter, while Metso was sold to fund more attractive investments.

Outlook

The two macro shocks during the quarter, Japan and China, serve as good reminders of extreme imbalances in the market today - notably the significant market concentration in a narrow sleeve of growth mega themes in the U.S. With the prospect of an increasing liquidity backdrop, stable economic prospects across most major international markets and the potential for Chinese policy support to remove a major drag from global growth, conditions remain supportive for a continued rotation to value and cyclical segments of the market.

In addition to increased market breadth, we also have seen the capex cycle around generative AI broaden out significantly as investors grapple with constraints around power and infrastructure. We are optimistic that this will continue to offer us opportunities to participate in this secular growth theme through stocks which remain overlooked and reasonably priced.

The improving economic and financial conditions notwithstanding, we recognize remaining event risks in light of the coming U.S. election and the geopolitical situation. As such, we have stayed balanced in our portfolio positioning, adding to utilities for defense and Chinese consumer companies for offense, with flexibility to pivot as things develop.

Portfolio Highlights

The ClearBridge International Value Strategy (MUTF:SBIEX) outperformed its MSCI EAFE benchmark during the third quarter. On an absolute basis, the Strategy had gains across 10 of the 11 sectors in which it was invested. The financials and industrials sectors were the main contributors, while the sole detractor was the energy sector.

On a relative basis, overall stock selection positively contributed to outperformance, partially offset by asset allocation effects. Specifically, stock selection in the consumer discretionary, financials, IT, consumer staples and health care sectors benefited returns. Conversely, stock selection in the materials sector, overweight allocations to the energy and IT sectors and an underweight to the utilities sector weighed on returns.

On a regional basis, stock selection in Europe Ex U.K., Japan and the U.K. and an overweight to North America were beneficial. Conversely, stock selection in North America, an overweight to emerging markets and an underweight to Asia Ex Japan detracted.

On an individual stock basis, Inditex, Fujitsu, Nexans (OTCPK:NXPRF), Hitachi and BAWAG Group were the leading contributors to absolute returns during the quarter. The largest detractors were Samsung Electronics (OTCPK:SSNLF), Shell, Gerresheimer (OTCPK:GRRMF), Marubeni (OTCPK:MARUY) and Galaxy Entertainment Group (OTCPK:GXYEF).

During the quarter, in addition to the transactions mentioned above, the Strategy initiated new positions in Willis Towers Watson (WTW) in the financials sector, Toho (OTCPK:THOOF) and Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY) in the communication services sector, THK (OTCPK:THKLY) in the industrials sector and Akeso (OTCPK:AKESF) in the health care sector. The Strategy exited positions in Bayerische Motoren Werke (OTCPK:BMWYY) and Galaxy Entertainment in the consumer discretionary sector, Nihon M&A (OTCPK:NHMAF) and Julius Baer (OTC:JBPCF) in the financials sector and Japan Airlines (OTCPK:JAPSY) in the industrials sector.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast: Crisis Response
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Crisis response

The world's second-largest economy appears to be slowing. China's economy grew at its weakest pace since the first quarter of 2023 amid a spiraling property sector, weak consumer demand, persistent deflationary risks and slowing export growth. This means that the government's full-year growth target of "around 5%" will not come easily unless more stimulus is poured into the ongoing crisis.

Numbers say what? The Chinese economy expanded by 4.6% between July and September, compared to the previous quarter's 4.7%; however, it was slightly better than estimates of 4.5%. On a quarterly basis, the economy grew by a seasonally adjusted 0.9%, below the 1.0% forecast. Additionally, data releases previously showed that exports slowed more than expected in September, growing 2.4% compared to 8.7% in August. China's September inflation rate has also come in weaker than expected, with producer prices falling to the lowest level in six months.

Mo' money: In a separate announcement, China's central bank on Friday officially kicked off the previously announced 800 billion yuan ($112B) stock market liquidity measures that allowed funds and brokers to tap central bank facilities to buy equities in a bid to shore up the country's capital markets. Additionally, keeping in mind the need to boost consumer spending, the People's Bank of China commented on Friday that interest rate cuts of 25 to 50 basis points before the end of 2024 could be in the pipeline depending on the economy's situation.

Market reaction: Initially, investors had cheered China's major stimulus package, first announced in late September. However, the absence of key details in subsequent press briefings left the markets dismayed. Today's announcement has helped restore investor confidence. China's blue-chip CSI 300 Index (SHSZ300) jumped 3.6% higher, while the broader Shanghai Composite (SHCOMP) and Hong Kong's Hang Seng Index (HSI) showed strength as well.

What else is happening...

World’s largest ETF hits historic milestone as it crosses $600B in AUM.

Netflix (NFLX) global streaming memberships maintain double-digit growth.

Raymond James' top picks in restaurant sector before Q3 results.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Netflix Jumps As Global Memberships Maintain Double-Digit Growth In Q3
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Key takeaways from Netflix's (NFLX) third-quarter earnings as stock gains. (00:23) Forward Air (FWRD) spiked into close after report of formal sales process. (01:58) Stripe (STRIP) in talks to acquire startup Bridge for $1B: Forbes. (03:05)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast.

Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX) is up 5% premarket after the company easily topped expectations for profits and user growth in its third-quarter earnings report.

Revenues grew 15% year-over-year, to $9.825B, and the company said it expected 15% growth for 2024 after similar expectations for the fourth quarter. Meanwhile, operating margin rose to 30% from a year-ago 22%, leading Netflix (NFLX) to bump its full-year expectations to 27% from 26%.

After some concerns in 2022 about membership growth topping out, the company has moved into a steady period of double-digit growth of users -- and this quarter, it again topped expectations, adding 5.07M global paid net members (a 14.4% growth rate) to land at 282.72M global paid memberships.

The story from previous quarters about an ad-supported plan proving even more profitable than expected continues. It has been nearly two years since Netflix launched its advertising business.

The company said, "Our ads plan allows us to offer a lower price point for consumers, which is proving to be popular: In Q3, it accounted for over 50% of sign-ups in our ads countries and membership on our ads plan grew 35% quarter over quarter."

The company notes that it is still "working to improve our monetization by refining our plans and pricing."

Forward Air (NASDAQ:FWRD) closed 6% higher on Thursday and is up 1% in premarket action after a report the company will initiate a formal sales process.

According to a FreightWaves report on Thursday, which cited people close to the matter, the company retained two investment banks for a sales process.

The news comes as activist investor Alta Fox earlier this month said it was pushing for an immediate strategic review and wants the company to start a formal sales process. AltaFox said it owns an over 3% stake in Forward Air (FWRD).

The Alta Fox push comes after fellow activist Ancora Holdings in August urged Forward Air (FWRD) to start a strategic review and evaluate a sale. Ancora, which has a 4% stake in Forward Air, hinted that it could initiate a proxy contest to "replace several board members" if the company did not start a review.

Also in August, Clearlake Capital, Forward Air's second-largest shareholder with a 13.8% stake, said in a regulatory filing that it may engage with management, the board and others to discuss strategic alternatives.

Infrastructure payments company Stripe (STRIP) is in negotiations to acquire crypto stablecoin infrastructure startup Bridge for $1B.

Forbes reported Thursday, citing three people familiar with the talks that a deal, which either side could walk away from, is likely the largest for Stripe.

Potential hurdles include regulatory issues and compensation for employees.

Forbes said Bridge has received interest over a potential Series B funding round after raising $58M in total funding.

Neither Stripe nor Bridge immediately responded to requests for comment by Seeking Alpha.

More articles on Seeking Alpha:

History says the stock market's bull run could keep going for years – Carson’s Detrick

Janet Yellen warns Trump's proposed tariffs would reignite inflation

Nvidia's smothering market share leads BofA to raise EPS estimate

Catalyst watch:

The key metric to watch is organic sales growth, which is forecast to be up 2.2% year-over-year, split between incremental pricing and volume gains. The P&G update from management on the upcoming conference call could be of interest to peers Church & Dwight (CHD), Kimberly-Clark (KMB), Colgate-Palmolive (CL), and Clorox (CLX). Notably, Glatfelter (GLT) derives 16% of its revenue from P&G and could be impacted as well.

  • Seeking Alpha VP of Quantitative Strategy Steve Cress will speak at the Orlando MoneyShow at 1:15 pm.

Now let’s take a look at the markets as of 6 am. Ahead of the opening bell today, Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the green. Crude oil is up 0.2% at $70/barrel. Bitcoin is up 1% at $67,000.

In the world markets, the FTSE 100 is down 0.2% and the DAX is up 0.2%.

On today’s economic calendar:
